doc.title: Unified Asset Pipeline — internal media-processing platform (PRD)

pushing_hardest_on: Success metrics are output counts (teams onboarded, jobs/day), not outcomes
No build-vs-buy: a hosted transcode service is the obvious alternative and isn't weighed
No risks or migration pre-mortem for moving the third team off its legacy scripts

axes.reason: Real internal demand and a quantified pain signal, but no sizing of the firefighting cost beyond a rough estimate.
axes.quote: on-call engineers spend roughly a day a week firefighting failed batches
axes.what_id_want_to_see: The actual on-call hours lost per team per month, and the two requesting teams' own words on what breaks.

axes.reason: Described as a capability, not a falsifiable hypothesis — no if/then/because and no stated mechanism for why a shared API reduces firefighting.
axes.quote: Teams call one API instead of maintaining their own scripts.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: An if/then/because: if teams move to one API, on-call load drops by N because failures route to one owned service instead of three drifting scripts.

axes.reason: Clear IN and OUT lists, but no CUT history or v1 trade-offs named.
axes.quote: Out of scope: audio, live streaming, and a self-serve UI
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A CUT list: what was in an earlier draft and removed to make v1, and why.

axes.reason: Outputs dressed as outcomes — teams-onboarded and jobs/day measure activity, not the firefighting reduction the project exists to deliver; no baseline, counter-metric, or threshold.
axes.quote: We will measure success by the number of teams onboarded and the number of jobs processed per day.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: On-call hours reclaimed as the north-star: a baseline (today's day/week/team), a target, a timeframe, and a failure-rate counter-metric that must not regress.

axes.reason: Internal platform with no external price — the economics are reframed as build-vs-buy under Strategy.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: n/a — see Strategy for the build-vs-buy question.

axes.reason: Build-vs-buy is unaddressed: a hosted transcode/thumbnail service is the obvious alternative to a home-built platform and is never weighed.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A build-vs-buy comparison against a hosted media-processing service, with the opportunity cost of the platform team's time.

axes.reason: Concrete staged internal rollout with a design partner first and parity sign-off gating each migration.
axes.quote: Onboard the team that asked first as a design partner, then the second team, then migrate the third off
axes.what_id_want_to_see: Already strong — a date or trigger for each stage would make it fully committed.

axes.reason: Stage is stated and the scope matches it — a reliability-driven consolidation of capabilities already in production, not premature discovery.
axes.quote: This is a platform consolidation of capabilities all three teams already run in production
axes.what_id_want_to_see: Already strong — explicit reliability targets (SLO) would complete the stage-fit picture.

axes.reason: No AI/LLM feature is proposed; transcode/thumbnail/metadata are deterministic processing.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: n/a — no model-backed behavior to govern.

axes.reason: No risks section and no pre-mortem — the third team's migration off legacy scripts is the obvious failure mode and is left unexamined.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A pre-mortem: it is six weeks post-cutover and the third team rolled back — what happened? Each failure mode with a leading indicator and a mitigation.

weakest_claims.claim: Teams-onboarded and jobs-per-day are the right success measures.
weakest_claims.quote: We will measure success by the number of teams onboarded and the number of jobs processed per day.
followups: Both can hit target while on-call load is unchanged — what outcome do they actually proxy?
What is today's firefighting baseline, and what reduction counts as success?

weakest_claims.claim: One shared API removes the firefighting problem.
weakest_claims.quote: Teams call one API instead of maintaining their own scripts.
followups: What happens to on-call when the shared service itself fails — does one outage now hit all three teams?
What's the migration cost for the third team, and does it exceed a quarter of saved firefighting?

strengths: Disciplined scope with an explicit OUT list
Concrete staged rollout gated on parity sign-off
Honest stage framing — reliability consolidation, not discovery
must_dos: Replace output counts with an outcome metric (on-call hours reclaimed: baseline → target → threshold)
Weigh build-vs-buy against a hosted transcode service under Strategy
Add a pre-mortem for the legacy-script migration

limits: Cost and opportunity-cost figures are not visible in this excerpt — they may live in a linked budget doc
